dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for a Breathing Problems call at [CITY].
Hello, I'm EMT Alex. What were you doing when you called 911?
Acute shortness of breath and chest pressure, after days of worsening symptoms.
Tracey, can you tell me your full name, where you're right now, who I am, and tell me the year?
I'm Tracey, I'm in [CITY], you're EMT Alex, and this is 2019.
I see you're on oxygen-is it okay for me to listen to your lungs to get a better idea of what's going on?
Thanks for letting me try. Can we now check your oxygen saturation to see how well your body is oxygenating?
Your oxygen saturation is showing as 98%. That's pretty good.
Correct, you are on 5 liters via nasal cannula, which should help.
Tracey, do you mind if I take a pulse and briefly test your blood pressure?
Heart rate is 96, which is a bit high but stable.
You are maintaining a steady breathing rhythm, with oxygen levels in a good range.
I plan on measuring your blood pressure and checking blood flow in your arms.
Blood pressure is currently 161 over 108.
I need to do a quick recording of your heart's electrical activity to see if there are any irregularities. This will help confirm our next steps.
A 4-lead EKG is being placed. It helps us monitor your heart on a continuous basis.
The 12-lead EKG is completed and shows a sinus rhythm with no ST segment changes.
I saw in your record that you have a history of COPD and CHF. How long has your shortness of breath been going on?
It started off about two weeks ago and has gotten steadily worse for the past three days, my legs have also swollen up.
You mentioned something about your nurse adjusting your oxygen dosage-let me confirm that.
Yes. She increased it from 3 to 5 liters, and it helped a little with my breathing.
You also said you are experiencing chest discomfort as well-is that right?
Yeah, it definitely is. The pain is at a level nine on the scale.
Have you noticed if the chest pressure shifts to another part of your body or if it just sits in your chest?
It stays centered in the middle of my chest.
It appears your legs are quite swollen, which is a fairly typical sign in CHF given your medical condition and weight.
And just to repeat, you are alert and aware of yourself, your location, the time, and what's going on here. AoX4.
Tracey, I need to lift your legs gently for a better look at the swelling-could you help me lift them?
Why don't we now discuss some of your other health conditions? What diagnoses have you received from your doctors?
I've had CHF, COPD, atrial fibrillation, high blood pressure, anxiety, and depression.
Great, thank you. What medications are you currently taking? Can you take me through the names?
I take Furosemide, Amiodarone, Metoprolol, Albuterol, Percocet for pain, Diazepam for anxiety, Citalopram for depression, Gabapentin, Budesonide, Warfarin, and Polyethylene Glycol.
I noted a few meds there-can you tell me if you're allergic to any of those or have any other allergies?
I'm allergic to Lisinopril. Other than that, I have No Known Allergies (NKA).
I'm going to start an IV access here to keep a line open in case we need to give medications quickly. Would that be okay with you?
The IV was successfully placed in your left arm with a 20-gauge needle and placed on a saline lock for now.
Now, we're going to give you some Aspirin (ASA) and one Nitroglycerin (NTG) tablet to help with your chest pain. Do you agree to this?
Your pulse is still at 95 right after the NTG.
Blood pressure hasn't really changed after the NTG, reading at 167 over 103.
Oxygen saturation remains good at 100% with 6 liters on the nasal cannula.
The blood glucose is at 118 mg/dL, a normal value. I'll do another quick check to be safe.
Respiratory rate is still at 19 breaths per minute. I'll recheck shortly while we continue monitoring you closely.
There was a small effect on your blood pressure from the NTG, but we'll keep watching and won't do a second dose right now.
Based on what you've described and our findings so far, we're thinking it could be related to CHF and possibly angina. We'll follow emergency protocols accordingly.
We're following protocol 2-3 as per our standing orders for suspected cardiac and pulmonary issues. I've recorded the administration of ASA and NTG.
We're going to move you onto the stretcher now-can you assist by supporting your legs?
I can help with that. Please, let's go slow because I've been having trouble with my legs.
We've carefully placed you onto the stretcher and made sure you're secure. Your IV and oxygen are still connected.
You haven't indicated a preference, but do you have a hospital you'd like us to transport you to?
[COMPANY_NAME] is where I would want to be taken.
Got it-[COMPANY_NAME] is our destination. Let's get rolling and be sure you're comfortable.
During this transport, we'll maintain your 6 liters of oxygen and keep monitoring for any change to your condition.
Before we get moving too far, I'll give vitals another look.
Pulse is still at 95 and BP hasn't changed-it stays at 167 over 103.
Your saturation is now at 100% while on 6 L of oxygen. Lungs can't be heard well due to body structure.
Wait-our second blood glucose reading is 0. Let's verify if that's correct or if the meter might be off.
A second reading showed the same 0-looks like device-related. Same as first result-no real difference.
Just checking-where is your chest pain now on the scale from one to ten?
Same as before, a nine. It still feels sharp and stays in the middle.
Let's get a final accounting. Tell me, have you had anything to eat or drink in the last 12 hours or so?
I haven't had anything for about 12 hours now-I just haven't felt like eating.
Leading up to your turnout, is there anything that might have changed recently that you think triggered all of this?
Not really, but I've had a rough time with fatigue and have stopped doing my regular exercises recently.
Let me verify-your nurse increased your oxygen to 5 liters last week because your condition was worsening, right?
Yes, she noticed the swelling and shortness of breath and thought that's why oxygen needed to go up.
Just to be 100% clear-no one else has given you any other medications beyond what you were already on.
No, just the nurse adjusting the oxygen. That's all.
Perfect, so we know exactly what medications you're on-no fear of overlap or duplication.
And for now, we are continuing on the same oxygen setting that was recommended.
Tracey, we need to place you onto the ambulance stretcher for transport. Can you again assist with your legs?
I can help-just as before, move slowly to avoid stressing it too much.
Your legs have been carefully lifted onto the cot and securely positioned. The IV and oxygen are still in place and working.
We've reached our destination of [COMPANY_NAME]-it's now time to get you inside.
All documentation has been taken care of and everything is ready for the hospital.
Tracey, we are transporting a 46-year-old female with suspected CHF, possible angina, and signs consistent with pulmonary edema.
We are gently lowering you onto the hospital bed and securing it like before.
I feel much better now that I'm being seen. I was really getting worried.
Once at the hospital, the doctors and nurses will take a closer look and continue the care we've started.
We've administered Aspirin (ASA) and Nitroglycerin (NTG) for your symptoms and have established IV access. You're NKA.
Vital signs upon arrival were 167/103, pulse at 95, and oxygen saturation at 100%.
Allergies: you are allergic to Lisinopril and otherwise NKA.
You were fully alert, no signs of trauma-we arranged transport with the ER nurse and updated them on the readings.
Your respiratory rate was recorded at 12 breaths per minute.
Everything's in order-Tracey, you're in good hands now.
